perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that until the expiry of eighteen (18) months from the date of this Agreement, each Party or its parent and affiliated companies (herein, collectively a "Party"), will not, and each Party will direct its parent and affiliated companies not to, except with the prior written consent of the other Party (i) purchase, acquire, obtain or hold (or offer or agree to purchase, acquire, obtain or hold) beneficial ownership of any of the securities of the other Party; (ii) make, or in any way participate in any solicitation of proxies to vote, or seek to advise or influence any person with respect to the voting of any of the other Party's securities; (iii) enter into, or agree to enter into or seek or propose any merger, consolidation, business combination, tender or exchange offer, sale or purchase of assets or securities, dissolution, liquidation, restructuring, recapitalization or similar transaction involving the other Party; (iv) act, alone or together with any person, to control or influence the management or the Board of Directors or other Party; or (v) advise, encourage, or assist any person in the acquisition of beneficial ownership of any of the other Party's securities or control of the other Party or a significant portion of its significant assets
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that for eighteen (18) months from this Agreement, each Party will generally refrain from, and will advise its affiliates to generally refrain from, without the other Party's consent: (i) purchasing securities of the other Party; (ii) participating in proxy solicitations regarding the other Party's securities; (iii) proposing mergers or similar transactions involving the other Party; (iv) acting to influence the management or board of the other Party; or (v) assisting others in acquiring control of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will not' with 'will generally refrain from' weakens the standstill provision. This creates ambiguity about the extent to which a party is prohibited from engaging in these activities. The term 'generally refrain' is not legally precise and could allow a party to argue that certain actions, while technically violating the spirit of the agreement, do not violate the letter of the agreement because they were not a general pattern of behavior.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Williams Act (15 U.S.C. § 78m(d)) regulates tender offers and acquisitions of securities. While this section is related to a standstill agreement, it is meant to prevent the acquisition of another company's shares. The 'generally refrain' language weakens this provision. If a party acquires more than 5% of the company's stock, the 'generally refrain' language could be seen as circumventing the purpose of the Williams Act to allow stakeholders to know when significant changes are being made to another company.

Periodical and other reports (a) Reports by issuer of security; contents Every issuer of a security registered pursuant to section 78l of this title shall file with the Commission, in accordance with such rules and regulations as the Commission may prescribe as necessary or appropriate for the proper protection of investors and to insure fair dealing in the security- (1) such information and documents (and suc
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will not' to 'will generally refrain from' weakens the contractual obligation. While the scraped snippets primarily concern reporting requirements under the Securities Exchange Act (15 USC 78m), the Law Explanation argues this change could circumvent the Act's purpose by allowing acquisitions of up to 5% of a company's stock without triggering disclosure requirements, due to the ambiguous 'generally refrain' language. The original text provided a definitive prohibition, while the modified text creates an ambiguity that could be exploited to undermine the standstill agreement. The legal obligation is now unclear, which is a contradiction.
